does: This paper introduces the Kerimov-Alekberli model, an information-geometric framework that connects non-equilibrium thermodynamics with stochastic control for AI safety. The core contribution establishes a formal isomorphism between thermodynamic principles and control theory, representing systemic anomalies as deviations from a Riemannian manifold. The framework uses Kullback-Leibler divergence as its primary metric with dynamic thresholds derived from the Fisher Information Metric, grounded in the Landauer Principle to show that adversarial perturbations perform measurable physical work by increasing informational entropy. The authors validate their approach on the NSL-KDD dataset and unmanned aerial vehicle simulations, demonstrating real-time anomaly detection with strong performance metrics including high accuracy and low false positive rates.
connects: This work builds on longstanding efforts to formalize AI safety through mathematical foundations, extending prior research that applies information geometry to machine learning and control systems. It responds to the challenge of bridging abstract safety concepts with rigorous physical principles, drawing on the thermodynamic perspective that has emerged in recent years for understanding learning dynamics and system behavior. The framework synthesizes stochastic control theory, which has deep roots in optimal control, with information-geometric methods popularized through information theory and differential geometry approaches to statistical learning. The use of Kullback-Leibler divergence and Fisher Information metrics reflects established tools in information theory and statistical mechanics, while the Landauer Principle connection ties classical thermodynamic bounds to computational processes, extending earlier work linking entropy and computation.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing physically-grounded safety guarantees in autonomous systems by providing formal tools to detect and characterize adversarial perturbations as thermodynamic processes. The approach could enable new methods for constructing robust control systems with provable safety properties derived from physical laws rather than empirical heuristics. Future work might leverage this thermodynamic perspective to design adversarially-robust learning algorithms, create energy-aware safety constraints for autonomous agents, or develop formal verification methods that exploit the Riemannian geometry of safe operating regions. The connection to physical work and entropy could also inform the design of inherently safer AI architectures and provide measurable metrics for alignment beyond traditional loss functions, facilitating real-time monitoring and intervention in deployed autonomous systems.
